# Extracao: transcription_pdf_M2_SampleQuestionsForSellerSitdown

- **Fonte:** transcription_pdf_M2_SampleQuestionsForSellerSitdown.txt
- **Tipo:** TXT
- **Tamanho:** 0.01 MB

---

Questions:
-- “Why are you selling the business?”
Follow-up question:
-- “Okay, so you’re selling because of [repeat the reason they gave you.]
And are you looking to sell immediately for any reason? Or, do you have
time to move at a conservative pace?”
Goal: Make sure they're truly ready to sell. And if they're distressed or if they're on solid
ground with options. If they're distressed and need to sell fast, it means you’ll have more
leverage in the purchase price and terms. If they're able to move slowly, it means that
they're less desperate and therefore, finding the right time will take more time, asking
great questions and creativity.
__________________________________________________
Question:
-- “Can you tell me a little bit about your original vision and motivation for starting the
business? What’s the origin story?”
Goal: Learn the first-hand account of the business's history and build a relationship with
the seller by intently listening to them talk about their baby.
__________________________________________________
Question:
-- “What do you want for the business, it’s employees and it’s customers when you sell
it?”
Goal: Find out what they hope their legacy is. And build out the buyer-archetype they're
looking for so you can determine if you’re both aligned. The seller’s answer should give you
important phrases and outcomes that, if you’re aligned with him on, you should continue
to speak to through the deal-making process.
__________________________________________________
Sample Questions for Seller Sitdown
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 4 --

Questions:
-- “Are you the sole owner of the business?”
Follow-up if there is more than one owner: “Is the other owner planning on
staying in the business after you sell?”
Follow-up if the other owner(s) are planning on staying: “Do you know
roughly, their exit plans? Do you know how long they are looking to
continue in the business?”
Goal: Determine if the deal is more complicated than you thought or want. If an owner
intends on staying in the business for a long time, it makes for more complicated terms and
management after the sale.
__________________________________________________
Questions:
-- “How many employees do you have?”
-- “How many superstar employees do you have?”
Follow-up question: “Who manages your employees?”
-- “Are any employees unionized? Any special licenses necessary for employees?”
Follow-Up if they are unionized or there is special licensing: “What does that
mean for your business’s operations?”
Goal: Find out the staffing and management requirements. Determine if there’s a non-
owner in place who manages the employees or whether you’ll have to yourself as the new
owner. Learning about how many superstar employees can tell you about the seller’s
business judgement. If he tells you they're all superstars or the majority are, it’s a signal
he’s a soft lead and that there might be a lot of upside from great management. On the
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 4 --

other hand, if he’s more realistic and says there are 2 or 3 superstars, you can start thinking
about retaining or incentivizing superstars to stay post-sale. And filter the business based
on large headwinds like unionization or licenses that might be too burdensome.
__________________________________________________
Questions:
-- “Do you own your land/buildings/equipment or do you rent them?”
Follow-up if the land/buildings are owned: “Are you looking to sell those? Or
lease them to your buyer?”
Follow-up if the facilities/land are leased: “What are the current lease
terms?”
Goal: You’re trying to see if there are assets that can be leveraged in the financing. Or,
terms that the seller is able to be flexible on to lessen the out of pocket cash necessary to
purchase the business.
__________________________________________________
Questions:
-- “Who does most of your revenue come from? You don’t have to give me specific
company/customer names -- but I’m interested to hear you speak about the revenue
centers you’ve built.”
Follow-up if the seller does NOT speak to the specific % of revenue that that
customer is responsible for: “That’s great. And, roughly, how much revenue
would you say that customer is responsible for? 5%? 50%? Some other %?”
Follow-up if the answer is one customer accounts for a quarter or more of
the business: “Understood. And, what’s the term-length of that customer's
contract?”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 4 --

Follow-up: “Ok, so roughly X% of your revenue comes from [answer
seller gave you] and that customer is under a [repeat contract terms
seller gave you.] And how much of your company’s overall revenue is
recurring vs. new sales?”
-- “What’s the geographic makeup of your customers?”
-- “What’s your #1 customer acquisition method?”
Goal: You’re trying to understand how fragile or robust the business's customer base is.
You’re looking for red flags like the business being dependent on one customer who is
under a very short term contract -- because that means the business is very vulnerable to
that customer leaving. You’re also demonstrating expertise in managing a business and
looking at weak-spots. If the seller has a highly concentrated customer base… and NO
contracts or no long-term contracts in place, too, you’ve inquired that information without
seeming condescending. The wrong way to ask about customer contract is, “and is that
customer under long-term contract?” because you’re implying that the seller has put his
company in a risky position.
__________________________________________________
Questions:
-- “Who are your biggest competitors?”
Follow-up: “And what do you think your business does better than them?”
Goal: You’re trying to get first-hand competitor analysis from the seller. In getting that,
you’re trying to gauge the owner's judgement of his business and the industry. Remember,
you can and should be doing your own industry research. And if you’re prepared with that
research prior to the meeting, you can match up what you’ve found with the seller’s
answers. If the seller has an unrealistic analysis of how his business beats the competition…
yet, his answers about revenue and management sound good to you, it might be a sign
there could be upside from simply adding 10X operational/strategic competency. If his
answers are in-line with your research, then, the upside will come from fresh thinking and
a bold vision for expanding the business.
_________________________________________________
